1.exposition: The first of the seven messages sets the pattern the rest will follow: an address to the angel of the church, a self-portrait of Christ drawn from the opening vision, the searching “I know your works”, a commendation, a complaint, a call, and a promise to the one who conquers. To Ephesus, the greatest city of Asia, the risen Lord presents himself as the one who holds the seven stars and walks among the golden lampstands — never absent from his churches, holding their ministers in his hand. He commends a church of tireless work, patient endurance, and vigilant discernment, one that tested false apostles and would not tolerate the Nicolaitans (vv.2–6). Yet against all of it stands one grave charge: “you have abandoned the love you had at first”. Orthodoxy and labour cannot compensate for cooled love; the remedy is to remember, repent, and do the first works — or the lampstand will be removed from its place (v.5). The letter closes with the first of the great overcomer promises: the tree of life in the paradise of God (v.7).
1.original_language: Two words open the vocabulary the seven letters will share. The overcomer is ho nikōn — “the one who conquers,” a present participle of continuous action: not one who has triumphed once, but one who keeps on prevailing in the spiritual conflict; each letter ends with a promise to him (v.7). The Nikolaitans (v.6) name a party whose deeds Christ hates — traditionally traced to a Nicolas, or read as a symbolic coinage (“conquerors of the people”) — teachers of the same accommodation to idol-food and immorality later tied to Balaam (vv.14–15). Ephesus's discernment is measured by what it will not bear.
1.historical_context: Ephesus was the foremost city of the Roman province of Asia, home to perhaps a quarter-million people and to the temple of Artemis, one of the seven wonders of the ancient world, whose pilgrim trade filled the city (as Acts 19 attests). Paul had laboured here three years (Acts 20:31), and early tradition placed the apostle John's later years in the city. This long-established, well-taught church had weathered waves of travelling teachers — hence its tested rejection of those “who call themselves apostles and are not” — but decades of vigilance had cost it something warmer.
1.christ: “Him who holds the seven stars in his right hand, who walks among the seven golden lampstands” (v.1) — the self-title is lifted straight from the vision of chapter 1: Christ holds the churches' messengers and moves in the midst of the churches themselves, never an absent Lord. The closing promise is equally christological: the tree of life “in the paradise of God” reaches back to Genesis 2:9 and forward to Revelation 22:2 — the access to life that Adam forfeited, restored to the conqueror through the one who himself hung upon a tree (Galatians 3:13).

verses.note: The message opens with Christ's self-designation from the vision: he holds the seven stars and walks among the seven golden lampstands. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the fixed form each of the seven letters keeps — an address to the angel, that is, the minister, of the church, then a title of Christ suited to what he must say. On the scope of the seven the witnesses divide: Barnes and Robertson take them as literal, historical congregations of Asia, each with its own real condition; the latin-fathers Victorinus and Bede read the number seven as also embracing the whole church, and in the prophetic tradition even successive ages — real churches that are at once representative of the church entire.

“I know your works, your toil and your patient endurance.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson hear the searching, perfect knowledge of Christ, commending their diligence, their hard labour, and their steadfast endurance. More, they “cannot bear those who are evil,” and have “tested those who call themselves apostles and are not, and found them false” — Barnes and Ellicott mark the church's zeal for purity, testing the spirits as 1 John 4:1 commands and exposing the pretenders as liars. Discernment is here a virtue, not a fault.

The commendation deepens: they have borne up for my name's sake, and have not grown weary. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note the motive that ennobles the toil — it was borne for Christ, not for self — and the perseverance that had not flagged under hardship. The praise of an unwearied faithfulness only makes the single reproof that follows the more startling.

“But I have this against you, that you have abandoned the love you had at first.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the turn: after all the praise, one serious fault mars the record. reformed Gill draws the fine distinction — not their love to God and Christ, which can never be wholly lost, but the fervent, early love, grown cold and languid, their love to one another among it. puritan Matthew Henry weighs the gravity: love is the heart of religion, and neither orthodoxy nor labour nor patience can make up for its decline.

The cure is threefold: “Remember… repent… and do the works you did at first.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott trace the steps — recollection of the height from which they fell, repentance, and a return to the deeds that first love once produced; feeling must issue in action. puritan Henry presses the threat that seals it: unless they repent, Christ will “remove your lampstand from its place” — the loss of the church's very standing, the withdrawal of his presence and gospel light. The warning is grave, yet conditioned wholly on repentance.

“Yet this you have: you hate the works of the Nicolaitans, which I also hate.” Amid the reproof a word of praise returns. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott identify the Nicolaitans as a party of licentious, idolatrous teaching, the error later joined to Balaam (vv.14–15); puritan Henry marks the sweetness of the agreement — the church hates what Christ hates, an abhorrence of evil deeds that is itself Christlike. latin-fathers Victorinus adds a caution: such praise carries a danger, that those so commended not be elated by their works and fall into neglect.

“He who has an ear, let him hear what the Spirit says to the churches.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the refrain that closes every letter, echoing Christ's own frequent call (Matthew 11:15): what is said to one church is said to all, the Spirit speaking through the message. To the conqueror comes the first promise — eastern Cyril and the grammatical voices read the tree of life “in the paradise of God” as eternal life and restored access to God's presence, Eden regained and pointing on to the final city (Revelation 22:2).

witnesses.point: The seven are literal Asian congregations, each addressed in a fixed pattern; the commendation of works, toil, and endurance is genuine before the single reproof is named.
The abandoned “first love” is not love to Christ, which can never be wholly lost, but the cooled fervency of early devotion and love to one another.
Love is the heart of religion; the threat to remove the lampstand is the threat to the church's very standing and Christ's presence with it.
By their number the seven churches represent the whole church; and those commended must beware being elated by their works and slipping into neglect.

external.name: G.K. Beale
external.point: The threatened removal of the lampstand inverts the opening image of Christ walking among the lampstands: to lose love is to forfeit the church's very identity as a light-bearing witness.

1.application: A church can be busy, sound, and vigilant, and still be dying at the root. Christ does not rank the loss of first love beneath its doctrine; he names it the fault that endangers the lampstand itself. The way back is not new zeal but old love — remembered, repented of, and returned to in the first works.

8.exposition: To Smyrna, the persecuted and impoverished church, Christ writes the shortest of the seven letters and one of only two with no complaint. He names himself “the first and the last, who died and came to life” — precisely the title a community facing death most needs. He knows their affliction and their poverty, and in the same breath overturns the world's account of them: “but you are rich.” He knows too the slander of those who claim to be Jews and are “a synagogue of Satan.” No rebuke follows, only a summons to courage: the devil is about to test them with prison and a bounded tribulation of “ten days,” and they must “be faithful unto death” for “the crown of life.” The conqueror, though the first death should take him, “will not be hurt by the second death.” To a church that looks poor and defeated, every word is comfort.
8.original_language: The reward is a stephanos (v.10) — the victor's wreath awarded to the athlete who finishes the contest, not the royal diadēma; in a city famed for the “crown” of porticoes on its acropolis, the promised crown is life itself and incorruptible. The “ten days” (hēmerōn deka) denote a real but measured and limited affliction, not an arbitrary span. And the “second death” — glossed later as the lake of fire (Revelation 20:14) — is the final, irreversible death, from which the martyr who suffers only the first is forever safe.
8.historical_context: Smyrna (modern Izmir) was among the loveliest and most Rome-loyal cities of Asia; it had raised a temple to the goddess Roma as early as 195 BC and later one to the emperor, so that the imperial cult pressed hard on any who would not offer the required sacrifice. Its Jewish community was sizeable and, the letter says, actively hostile — informing against the messianic believers. Here, decades later, the aged bishop Polycarp, reckoned a disciple of John, would be martyred, answering the demand to curse Christ with the words, “Eighty-six years I have served him, and he has done me no wrong.” The letter's theology is his epitaph written in advance.
8.christ: “The words of the first and the last, who died and came to life” (v.8) — the title is drawn from the vision (Revelation 1:17–18) and chosen for its hearers. “The first and the last” is a divine name borrowed from Isaiah 44:6, spoken there of the LORD, now claimed by Christ: the eternal one addresses those about to die. And “who died and came to life” is the whole paschal mystery in four words, offered to a church that may be called to walk the same road — his resurrection the pledge of theirs.

verses.note: The second letter opens with the title fitted to a suffering church: Christ is “the first and the last, who died and came to life.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson trace it to the vision of chapter 1 and to the divine name of Isaiah 44:6, affirming Christ's eternity. puritan Henry marks how exactly the title suits the hearers: a church facing martyrdom is addressed by the one who himself died and rose, so that his resurrection becomes the pledge of their own.

“I know your tribulation and your poverty — but you are rich.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson savour the paradox: outwardly afflicted and poor, the Smyrnaeans are rich in faith and grace and heaven's treasure (cf. James 2:5); Christ's reckoning, not the world's, sets their true worth. On “the synagogue of Satan,” reformed Gill notes that these were Jews by birth and circumcision who were “not Jews in the true spiritual sense” — their slander of the church marking them, for all their claim, as the devil's instrument.

“Do not fear what you are about to suffer.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott read the foretold trial soberly: the devil, working through the persecutors, will imprison some “that you may be tested,” and the “ten days” set a real but limited term to the tribulation. Then the charge and its promise — puritan Henry: “be faithful unto death,” even to the point of dying, “and I will give you the crown of life”; faithfulness carried through martyrdom is crowned with the victor's wreath of eternal life.

The refrain, and the conqueror's promise: “the one who conquers will not be hurt by the second death.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott define the second death as the final, eternal death — the lake of fire of Revelation 20:14 — as distinct from the first, bodily death. puritan Henry draws the comfort exactly suited to Smyrna: though the first death take the faithful martyr, the second has no power over him at all; his life is secure.

witnesses.point: The poverty is outward only; by heaven's accounting the persecuted, materially poor church is truly rich in faith and grace.
The “synagogue of Satan” were Jews by birth but not in the true spiritual sense, functioning as slanderers and accusers of the church.
“Faithful unto death” means fidelity even to dying; the crown of life is the martyr's reward, and the second death cannot touch the one who conquers.

external.name: Richard Bauckham
external.point: In Revelation conquering is never by force but by faithful witness maintained even to death; the martyr's apparent defeat is the very form the victory takes, which the crown of life rewards.

8.application: Smyrna receives no rebuke and no rescue from suffering — only the promise that the suffering is measured, and that the worst the world can do reaches no further than the first death. To a church that looks poor and defeated, Christ says: you are rich, and you are safe. Faithfulness, not survival, is the victory he crowns.

12.exposition: Pergamum lived, Christ says, “where Satan's throne is” — and to it he presents himself as the one “who has the sharp two-edged sword,” the word that judges. He commends a church that held fast his name and did not deny the faith even when Antipas, his faithful witness, was killed among them. Yet steadfastness under outward pressure had not purchased vigilance within: “you have some there who hold the teaching of Balaam” and “the teaching of the Nicolaitans” — teachers counselling the very accommodation to idol-food and immorality that Balaam once used to ensnare Israel. What Ephesus hated, Pergamum tolerated. The call is sharp: “repent,” or Christ will come and war against them “with the sword of my mouth.” To the conqueror he promises the hidden manna, and a white stone bearing a new name known only to its receiver.
12.original_language: The promised token is a psēphos (v.17) — a small stone or pebble, used in the ancient world as a juror's vote (white for acquittal, black for condemnation), as a ticket of admission, and as a victor's token; the white stone gathers all these into a sign of the overcomer's acquittal, welcome, and honour. The “new name… which no one knows except the one who receives it” marks an intimate, personal standing with Christ. Against the guild-feasts of idol-food, the “hidden manna” offers the true, secret nourishment the world cannot give — the manna once hidden in the ark, now fulfilled in Christ himself.
12.historical_context: Pergamum, a former capital of Asia, rose on a dramatic acropolis crowned by the great altar of Zeus and, from 29 BC, the first temple of the imperial cult in the province — the symbolic centre of emperor-worship, and the likeliest referent of “Satan's throne.” Its proconsul held the ius gladii, the very power of execution. Into this concentrated pagan pressure the letter's “doctrine of Balaam” fits precisely: as Balaam counselled Balak to seduce Israel into idolatry and immorality at Peor (Numbers 25; 31:16), so the Pergamene teachers urged accommodation to the idol-feasts woven through civic and trade-guild life.
12.christ: “The words of him who has the sharp two-edged sword” (v.12) — in the vision the sword proceeds from Christ's mouth (Revelation 1:16), the word of God that cuts and judges (Hebrews 4:12). In a city where the proconsul bore the right of the sword over life and death, the title is a quiet counter-claim: final judgment belongs not to Rome but to Christ. And the “hidden manna” (v.17) is christological — the bread hidden in the ark was the type of the true bread from heaven that Christ declares himself to be (John 6:35).

verses.note: The third letter presents Christ “who has the sharp two-edged sword.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott identify the sword, from the vision of chapter 1, as the word of Christ that proceeds from his mouth and judges (Hebrews 4:12) — a title deliberately fitted to a church troubled by false teaching. puritan Henry notes that the same sword prepares for both commendation and rebuke: the word held ready will, if need be, be turned against the false teachers within (v.16).

“I know where you dwell, where Satan's throne is.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson explain the phrase by the city's concentrated idolatry and emperor-worship — a stronghold of evil and persecution. Yet there the church “held fast my name” and “did not deny my faith,” even when “Antipas my faithful witness” was slain among them. puritan Henry makes the martyrdom the proof of both the danger and the genuineness: fidelity that held firm through the death of one of their own is fidelity indeed.

“But I have a few things against you: you have some there who hold the teaching of Balaam.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott recall the history (Numbers 25; 31:16): unable to curse Israel, Balaam taught Balak to seduce them into idol-feasts and fornication. reformed Gill presses the parallel — the church harboured members following that wicked prophet's method, enticing God's people into the same idolatry and immorality. The fault is not holding the error but tolerating those who do.

“So also you have some who hold the teaching of the Nicolaitans.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson link the two parties: the Nicolaitan doctrine is the same licentious corruption as Balaam's, and Pergamum tolerated what Ephesus had hated (v.6). Christ adds, “which I hate” — puritan Henry marks the gravity: to harbour what Christ hates is to share its guilt, unless it be repented of and put away.

“Repent; if not, I will come to you soon and war against them with the sword of my mouth.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note that the whole church is called to repent — meaning here to remove or discipline the false teachers, not merely to feel sorrow. puritan Henry sees the sword-title (v.12) now turned against the impenitent: if the church will not deal with the error, Christ himself will come and judge the teachers by the word of his mouth.

The refrain, then two promises to the conqueror: “the hidden manna,” and “a white stone, with a new name written on it.” reformed Gill reads the hidden manna as Christ himself, the true bread once hidden in the golden pot within the ark, now the spiritual food of the overcomer. On the white stone, grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson survey the views — a juror's stone of acquittal, a token of admission, a victor's prize — all converging on acceptance and honour; the secret new name marks the conqueror's own intimate, glorified standing with Christ.

witnesses.point: “Satan's throne” names Pergamum's concentrated idolatry and emperor-worship; the two-edged sword is the judging word that proceeds from Christ's mouth.
Antipas's martyrdom proves both the peril of the place and the reality of the church's steadfastness; to tolerate what Christ hates is to share its guilt.
The Balaam party enticed God's people into idolatry and immorality as Balaam once did; the hidden manna is Christ, the true bread hidden in the ark.
The white stone gathers several ancient uses — acquittal, admission, victory — all signifying the overcomer's acceptance and secret honour with Christ.

external.name: Craig R. Koester
external.point: Reads “Satan's throne” against Pergamum's role as the seat of the provincial imperial cult and the proconsul's power of the sword, so that Christ's sword of the mouth answers Rome's ius gladii with a higher and final judicial authority.

12.application: Pergamum shows that a church may stand bravely against the sword outside its walls and still fall to the compromise offered within them. Holding fast Christ's name under threat is not the same as guarding his truth in peace. The word that defends the faithful is the same word that will judge the teachers they tolerate — and Christ bids them wield it before he must.

18.exposition: The longest of the seven letters goes to the smallest city, and it is a study in the mixture of genuine growth and grave compromise. Christ comes as “the Son of God, who has eyes like a flame of fire and feet like burnished bronze” — the searching, judging Lord. He commends what Ephesus had lost: love, faith, service, and endurance, with works that are increasing, “your latter works exceeding the first.” Yet “you tolerate that woman Jezebel,” a self-styled prophetess seducing his servants into fornication and idol-food. She was given time to repent and would not; judgment falls on her and her followers, and “all the churches will know” that Christ “searches mind and heart.” To the faithful remnant who never learned “the deep things of Satan,” he lays “no other burden” but to “hold fast… until I come.” The conqueror is promised authority over the nations and, at the last, “the morning star” — Christ himself.
18.original_language: “Jezebel” (v.20) is almost certainly a symbolic name: as Ahab's queen imported Baal-worship into Israel (1 Kings 16:31), so this prophetess imports idolatry into the church. The sardonic phrase “the deep things of Satan” (v.24) most likely inverts her circle's own boast of knowing “the deep things” — Christ renaming their supposed depth as satanic. And when he “searches mind and heart” (v.23), the Greek is literally “the kidneys and hearts” (nephrous kai kardias), the Hebrew idiom for the innermost self that Scripture reserves to God alone (Jeremiah 17:10).
18.historical_context: Thyatira was the least of the seven cities but the busiest in trade — a town of dyers (Lydia, the seller of purple, was from here, Acts 16:14), bronze-workers, and potters, dominated more than any of the others by the craft guilds. Guild membership meant guild banquets, and those banquets meant meat offered to the patron deities; to refuse was to court economic ruin. “Jezebel's” teaching very likely offered a theological escape — perhaps that an idol is nothing, so its food may be safely eaten — letting believers keep their livelihoods while sharing the idol-feast. The letter's severity measures how much was at stake.
18.christ: “The Son of God, who has eyes like a flame of fire, and whose feet are like burnished bronze” (v.18) — the title “Son of God” appears in Revelation only here, pointedly, in a city where divine sonship was claimed for the emperor. The fiery eyes and glowing feet are the features of the heavenly figure of Daniel 10:6, applied to Christ and fitted to a letter of searching judgment. His claim to “search mind and heart” (v.23) is divine omniscience (Jeremiah 17:10); and the closing “morning star” (v.28) is Christ's own self-name — “the bright morning star” of Revelation 22:16 — so that the conqueror's prize is finally the Lord himself.

verses.note: The fourth letter presents “the Son of God, who has eyes like a flame of fire and feet like burnished bronze.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note that “the Son of God” affirms Christ's deity and authority, while the flaming eyes signify a penetrating, all-searching knowledge — fitted to a church about to be searched and judged (vv.20–23). The feet of glowing bronze, from the vision of chapter 1, are feet of strength and judgment, ready to tread down evil.

“I know your works — your love and faith and service and patient endurance, and that your latter works exceed the first.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark a commendation warmer than Ephesus received: Thyatira is rich in the practical graces, especially the love from which its works flow. puritan Henry catches the contrast exactly — where Ephesus had declined from its first love, Thyatira was growing, its later works surpassing the earlier; increasing fruitfulness, not decay.

“But I have this against you, that you tolerate that woman Jezebel, who calls herself a prophetess.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott read “Jezebel” as named for Ahab's idolatrous queen (1 Kings 16), a false prophetess promoting idolatry and immorality under a claim of inspiration. reformed Gill lays the fault squarely on the church: it should not have suffered her to teach, but used the keys of the kingdom and put her out; to wink at her doctrine was itself the sin.

“I gave her time to repent, but she refuses to repent of her sexual immorality.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the forbearance shown even to the corrupt: Christ granted the prophetess space and opportunity, an offer of grace preceding judgment. puritan Henry marks the hardening — impenitence after mercy seals the doom that follows; the delay was itself mercy, and its refusal justifies the sentence.

“Behold, I will throw her onto a sickbed, and those who commit adultery with her into great tribulation, unless they repent.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott hear the irony: the bed of her sin becomes a bed of affliction, her sin made the instrument of her punishment. puritan Henry notes the open door left even here — “unless they repent” extends mercy still to her followers; the threat is at the same time a call to turn.

“I will strike her children dead, and all the churches will know that I am he who searches mind and heart.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott distinguish “her children” — those wholly given to her corruption — from the followers who might yet repent (v.22); their judgment will teach all the churches that Christ searches the inmost being, the divine prerogative of Jeremiah 17:10. puritan Henry adds the principle it seals: “I will give to each of you according to your works.”

“But to the rest of you in Thyatira, who do not hold this teaching, who have not learned what some call the deep things of Satan, I lay on you no other burden.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott hear a sarcasm: the false teachers boasted of “the deep things,” which John names “the depths of Satan,” and the faithful had rightly kept clear of them. puritan Henry marks the gentleness — Christ imposes no new demand on the faithful, only that they keep what they already have.

“Only hold fast what you have until I come.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read the simple charge: the faithful need attain nothing new, only retain firmly the faith and works they already possess. puritan Henry sets the horizon — “until I come” — the perseverance is to last to Christ's coming; steadfastness, not novelty, is what he asks of the remnant.

“The one who conquers and keeps my works until the end, to him I will give authority over the nations.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott stress that the promise is to enduring faithfulness — not a momentary victory but works kept “to the end.” The reward, drawn from Psalm 2:8–9, is a share in Christ's own royal authority: the saints will reign with him over the nations.

“He will rule them with a rod of iron, as when earthen pots are broken in pieces, even as I myself have received authority from my Father.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson trace the citation to Psalm 2:9: a firm, irresistible rule that shatters opposition like a potter's vessels. eastern Cyril marks the source of the gift — “even as I received of my Father” — the conqueror's dominion is a participation in the very authority Christ received, a derived and shared sovereignty.

“And I will give him the morning star.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the crowning promise and the reigning interpretation: the morning star is Christ himself, who calls himself “the bright morning star” (Revelation 22:16); to be given the morning star is to possess Christ in his glory. eastern Cyril hears in the herald of dawn the pledge of eternal glory breaking upon the overcomer — Christ the true light given as the conqueror's everlasting portion.

“He who has an ear, let him hear what the Spirit says to the churches.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note the closing refrain — now placed after the promise, as in the last four letters — addressing everyone who will heed. The message to Thyatira is the Spirit's word to every church: what was spoken to one congregation is spoken, through the Spirit, to all, in every age.

witnesses.point: Thyatira grows where Ephesus declined, its latter works exceeding the first; yet even in judgment mercy is offered — “unless they repent.”
The church's sin was tolerating Jezebel to teach; it ought to have used the keys of the kingdom and excommunicated her rather than wink at her doctrine.
“Her children” are those wholly given to the corruption, distinct from followers who might repent; the morning star given to the conqueror is Christ himself.
The overcomer's rule over the nations is a share in the authority Christ received from the Father; the morning star is the dawn of eternal glory, Christ himself.

external.name: Grant R. Osborne
external.point: Takes “Jezebel” as a real prophetess within the church whose teaching justified participation in the guild-feasts, so that the letter's charge falls not on those who share her error but on a congregation that tolerates it.

18.application: Thyatira is the mirror-opposite of Ephesus: warm, loving, growing — and precisely for that reason tempted to a tolerance that will not name a deadly error. Love that will not confront is not the whole of love. Christ's flaming eyes see both the genuine growth and the fatal compromise, and of the faithful he asks only that they hold fast what they have. The one who does receives not merely authority over the nations, but the Morning Star himself.

_meta.title: The Messages to the Seven Churches — Ephesus, Smyrna, Pergamum, Thyatira
reflection: Ephesus kept its doctrine and its diligence but “abandoned the love it had at first” (v.4). Where has faithfulness in you hardened into mere correctness, and what would it look like to “do the first works” again (v.5)?

Smyrna is called “rich” in the middle of poverty and persecution (v.9) and told to be “faithful unto death” for the crown of life (v.10). How does Christ's accounting of wealth and victory differ from the world's — and from your own?

Pergamum stood firm against the sword outside its walls yet tolerated false teaching within, and Thyatira's very love made it slow to confront “Jezebel” (v.14; v.20). Where might faithfulness to Christ require you to stop tolerating what he hates?
